title: Residual Epistasis Networks: Structure-Conditioned Graph Neural Networks for Correcting Additive Protein Fitness Predictions
description: We propose Residual Epistasis Networks (REN), a framework that decomposes multi-mutant protein fitness prediction into an additive component from a protein language model (ESM-2) and a non-additive epistatic correction learned by a graph attention network operating over AlphaFold-predicted structural contact maps. By training the GNN only on the residual between PLM predictions and observed fitness, REN explicitly targets the epistatic signal that current methods miss.
motivation: Protein language models achieve strong zero-shot fitness prediction for single mutations but default to additive assumptions for multi-mutant variants, ignoring epistasis — where the combined effect of mutations differs from the sum of individual effects. Epistasis is pervasive in protein fitness landscapes and critical for protein engineering, directed evolution, and understanding genetic disease. Current approaches either combine sequence and structure end-to-end without separating additive from epistatic contributions, or require expensive molecular dynamics simulations. There is no method that explicitly learns the non-additive residual using scalable structural features from AlphaFold.
proposed_approach: REN operates in two stages: (1) compute ESM-2 zero-shot log-likelihood ratio scores for each mutation and sum them for multi-mutant variants (additive baseline, no training); (2) construct a graph from AlphaFold-predicted Cb-Cb contacts (<10A), with ESM-2 per-residue embeddings as node features and mutation identity encodings, then train a 3-layer graph attention network to predict the epistatic residual (observed fitness minus additive prediction). The GNN uses attention-weighted pooling over mutation sites and a 2-layer MLP head. This decomposition focuses the GNN's capacity on genuinely non-additive signal and provides interpretable attention weights that reveal structural determinants of epistasis.

related_work.title: Multi-Scale Representation Learning for Protein Fitness Prediction (S3F)
related_work.authors: Zuobai Zhang, Pascal Notin, Yining Huang, Aurelie Lozano, Vijil Chenthamarakshan, Debora Marks, Payel Das, Jian Tang
related_work.difference: S3F combines PLM and structural GNN end-to-end for general fitness prediction. REN explicitly decomposes into additive + epistatic components, training only the correction module.

related_work.title: A Protein Dynamics-Based Deep Learning Model Enhances Predictions of Fitness and Epistasis
related_work.difference: Uses dynamics-based GNN edges from MD simulations (expensive). REN uses static AlphaFold contacts, making it scalable to any protein with a predicted structure.

related_work.title: Protein Language Models Capture Structural and Functional Epistasis in a Zero-Shot Setting
related_work.authors: Ananthan Nambiar, Sayantani B. Littlefield, Carlos Cuellar, Rohit Khorana, Sergei Maslov
related_work.difference: Benchmarks PLMs on epistasis but does not propose a correction method. REN builds on their findings by adding an explicit structural correction.

related_work.title: SaProt: Protein Language Modeling with Structure-aware Vocabulary
related_work.authors: Jin Su, Chenchen Han, Yuyang Zhou, Junjie Shan, Xibin Zhou, Fajie Yuan
related_work.difference: Incorporates structure through 3Di tokens during pre-training. REN is a post-hoc correction that can augment any PLM including SaProt.

related_work.title: Epistatic Net allows the sparse spectral regularization of deep neural networks for inferring fitness functions
related_work.authors: Amirali Aghazadeh, Hunter Nisonoff, Orhan Ocal, et al.
related_work.difference: Uses sparse spectral regularization without PLM embeddings or structural information. REN leverages both PLM knowledge and 3D structure.

related_work.title: ProteinGym: Large-Scale Benchmarks for Protein Fitness Prediction and Design
related_work.authors: Pascal Notin, Aaron Kollasch, Daniel Ritter, et al.
related_work.difference: Provides the benchmark we evaluate on. Their analysis shows PLMs fail on strongly epistatic combinations — the gap REN addresses.

related_work.title: Addressing Epistasis in the Design of Protein Function (EpiNNet)
related_work.difference: Focused on computational protein design, not natural fitness landscapes. REN targets DMS-based fitness prediction.

related_work.title: Rapid Directed Evolution Guided by Protein Language Models and Epistatic Interactions (MULTI-evolve)
related_work.authors: Vincent Q. Tran, Matthew Nemeth, et al.
related_work.difference: A practical protein engineering pipeline. REN provides a general-purpose prediction framework with explicit structural epistasis modeling.

hypothesis: A graph attention network trained on AlphaFold structural contact maps with ESM-2 embeddings as node features can learn to predict the non-additive (epistatic) component of multi-mutant protein fitness, significantly improving prediction accuracy over additive PLM baselines on the ProteinGym multi-mutant benchmark, with larger gains for structurally proximal mutation pairs.
success_criteria: Statistically significant improvement (p < 0.05, paired Wilcoxon test) over additive ESM-2 on ProteinGym multi-mutant average Spearman correlation
Larger improvement on high-epistasis proteins (top quartile) than low-epistasis proteins
GAT attention weights on structurally proximal mutation pairs correlate with measured epistatic effects (Spearman rho > 0.3 on GB1)
Performance gains increase with mutation order (triples > doubles > singles)

feasibility_notes.compute: ESM-2 650M fits on 1x A6000 (48GB); GNN is lightweight (<10M params); total runtime ~7-8 hours
feasibility_notes.data: ProteinGym v1.3 provides 69 multi-mutant assays with 1.77M variants and pre-built CV folds; AlphaFold structures freely available
feasibility_notes.novelty_risk: Low — the individual components are well-established; the novel contribution is the explicit residual decomposition and structural correction framework
